nativeRange.habitat: Protected reef slopes, overhangs, and lower reef faces with reduced, diffused light and low to moderate water movement; commonly found plating across shaded ledges and deeper reef zones

decorPreferences: stable rockwork base for encrusting or plating growth
horizontal surface, overhanging ledge, or lower to mid rockwork placement
ample open space around the colony (10+ cm clearance from neighbors)

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Supplemental target feeding once or twice weekly with small meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, baby brine shrimp, or Reef Roids tends to benefit growth and coloration. Oxypora is reported to feed readily; a turkey baster or pipette for targeted delivery is generally advisable. Feeding often occurs at night when sweeper tentacles are extended.

goodWith: non-aggressive LPS corals placed well out of sweeper tentacle reach
avoidWith: corals within sweeper tentacle range (5–10 cm)
other Oxypora colonies without adequate spacing
aggressive LPS such as Galaxea or Hammer placed nearby
high-flow powerheads directed at colony surface

imageCandidates.notes: Oxypora convoluta by Jean-Paul Boerekamps. Sharp in-situ whole-colony disc showing the deep blue-purple oral center ringed by yellow-tipped tuberculate polyps and a scalloped plating margin; the most striking live Oxypora shot found.

Oxypora crassispinosa by Ryan McMinds. Sharp in-situ shot of vivid golden-yellow ruffled plates with pale scalloped margins and raised white polyp bumps; strong representation of a second genus member.
Oxypora lacera by Paul Muir, 3648x2736. Crisp close view of a laminar plate with scattered green oral disks and the diagnostic thin scalloped growing edge; the most commonly traded Oxypora species.

Oxypora crassispinosa by Diego Delso, 8688x5792. High-res in-situ whole-colony plating disc with grey-mauve tissue and dense tuberculate polyps; complements the tighter crassispinosa shot with full colony form.

Oxypora glabra by Paul Muir, 3648x2736. Sharp in-situ pink-to-cream plating colony with fine tuberculate texture and the thin white growing margin; adds a fourth genus member (some databases now place glabra in Echinophyllia).
Oxypora lacera by Paul Muir, 3648x2736. Second lacera individual showing the tan plate with white raised polyps and pronounced scalloped edge from a different angle.

summary: The Oxypora Coral is a large polyp stony coral — commonly abbreviated LPS — sold under the broad trade name 'Chalice Coral,' prized for vivid color morphs. It is generally considered intermediate in care and tends to do best under low to moderate light and gentle flow.
careNotes:
Chalice trade-umbrella note: 'Chalice Coral' is a broad commercial umbrella name applied to several genera in the aquarium hobby. The true chalice clade — Echinophyllia (see [echinophyllia-coral]) and Oxypora — both belong to family Lobophylliidae and are genuinely closely related. Mycedium (see [mycedium-coral]) was historically grouped with this clade but has since been reclassified to family Merulinidae; despite the shared chalice trade name and plating growth form, it is more closely related to Echinopora than to true chalice. Echinopora (see [echinopora-coral]) is also sold as chalice but belongs to family Merulinidae; the shared trade name reflects a convergent plating growth form, not a close phylogenetic relationship. Other genera sometimes marketed as chalice include Pectinia and Lithophyllon.

ORA Easter Egg Chalice: The best-known trade strain is the ORA Easter Egg Chalice (Oxypora sp.), recognized by its ultra-pink tissue with bright yellow oral disks. The original mother colony was sourced from a display tank at MACNA Orlando 2010 and has since been propagated through six or more aquaculture generations. Frags are available from a number of reef specialty vendors. The Easter Egg Chalice is generally considered among the more forgiving Oxypora morphs in captivity.

Taxonomy: The genus Oxypora was established by Saville-Kent in 1871. Five species are currently recognized: O. lacera (Verrill, 1864 — the most commonly encountered in trade), O. crassispinosa, O. convoluta, O. glabra, and O. egyptensis. Most aquarium specimens are sold at genus level (Oxypora sp.) or by color-morph trade name without species identification. The family placement is Lobophylliidae, order Scleractinia. Older sources may list the family as Pectiniidae, reflecting pre-2012 taxonomy.

Lighting: Oxypora is generally reported to prefer the lower end of the chalice lighting range — notably lower than Echinophyllia and significantly lower than SPS corals. New specimens should be acclimated over two to three weeks starting at lower light, as bleaching from excess PAR is a documented risk.

Flow: Low to moderate flow is generally advised — sufficient to oxygenate tissue and clear debris, but not strong enough to cause polyps to retract or prevent sweeper tentacles from extending during feeding.

Aggression: Oxypora extends sweeper tentacles at night, with reach typically reported at 5–10 cm. Placement must account for the full extent of nighttime extension; the 10 cm or more clearance from neighboring corals noted in placement preferences is a conservative working minimum.

Colony morphology: Colonies are thin-plated (laminar), commonly just 1–4 mm thick except near the attachment point. In the wild, O. lacera rarely exceeds 30–40 cm across; captive specimens typically reach 10–25 cm before tank constraints limit further expansion. The thin skeleton makes colonies comparatively brittle and porous — handle with care to avoid fracture.

Water quality: Stable parameters are important. Nitrate around 1–20 ppm and phosphate 0.01–0.1 ppm are documented as workable ranges. Calcium, alkalinity, and magnesium supplementation follow the same general approach as for other LPS corals.

Fragging: Oxypora is relatively straightforward to frag using bone cutters or a band saw. The thin skeleton means smaller frags (2–3 cm) can be viable, though care is needed to avoid cracking. Frags should be mounted on frag plugs or rubble with coral glue; under stable conditions, they typically begin encrusting and growing within a few weeks. Dipping new frags before introduction is advisable.
breedingNotes: Fragging is the primary propagation method in captivity. ORA has aquacultured Oxypora through multiple generations, and captive-propagated frags are available from a number of reef specialty vendors. O. lacera is documented as a hermaphrodite in the wild, releasing gametes for planktonic larval dispersal; deliberate sexual breeding is not practiced in home aquaria.

primary.name: Bulk Reef Supply – ORA Easter Egg Chalice Coral

additional.name: ReefChasers – Chalice Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked pH (8.0–8.4), alkalinity (8–9 dKH), calcium (430–460 ppm), PAR (50–125 low, 125–250 moderate), and care level (intermediate). Confirmed chalice umbrella genera and aggressive sweeper tentacle character. Temperature range 77–84°F factored into cross-source average.

additional.name: TankFacts – Oxypora crassispinosa Chalice
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (23–27°C), salinity (1.023–1.025), pH (7.8–8.4), alkalinity (8–12 dKH), calcium (380–450 ppm), magnesium (1200–1400 ppm), flow (low to moderate), and aggression. pH lower bound of 7.8 treated as an outlier; two-source consensus of 8.1–8.4 adopted.

additional.name: World Wide Corals – Proper Chalice Coral Care
additional.notes: Confirmed low PAR preference (50–100 PAR) for chalice genera, light to moderate flow, weekly target feeding with meaty foods, and multi-genera umbrella scope (Echinophyllia, Echinopora, Oxypora, Mycedium).

additional.name: ORA – Easter Egg Chalice product page
additional.notes: Confirmed placement (bottom–middle), growth form (thin plating, fast-growing), hardiness, and aquaculture origin history (mother colony from MACNA Orlando 2010 LiveAquaria display). Flow listed as moderate–strong — broader than other sources; mid-range ('medium') adopted as consensus.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Oxypora lacera
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Lobophylliidae, order Scleractinia), native range (Red Sea to Japan, Australia), depth range (~30 m), colony morphology (thin laminar plates, rarely >30 cm), and reproductive mode (hermaphrodite broadcast spawner). Not used for husbandry parameters.
